title: When a Parent Dies
subtitle: A reading on the long arc of parent-loss, in the months and the years that follow
excerpt: A twenty-minute reading on the long arc of losing a parent. The shock, the first year, the second year, the slow shape of a life that has them in it as a different presence. Used in any year after the loss.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The continuing-bonds frame draws on the work of Klass, Silverman, and Nickman (1996) and on later grief research.

practice.whenToUse: Read in any year after the loss of a parent. Especially useful in the first year, around anniversaries, and at the points where the grief shifts shape.

content.text: Losing a parent is the loss that most adults live through. It is also the loss that most people are least prepared for. The shock is real, and so is the long arc that follows.

Older grief writing said the work was to let go. Newer grief writing says the work is to find the new shape of the bond. The parent is not coming back. They are also not erased. They are now a different presence in your life.
The shock
The first weeks are shock. The body is sorting things. There is no normal sleep, no normal hunger, no normal sense of time. Everything you do for the first month, you are doing inside the shock.

The work of the shock weeks is small. Eat what you can. Sleep when you can. Let the people who can carry things carry them. Do not make big choices in the first month. Most of what you feel is not what you will feel in a year.
The first year

The first year is the year of firsts. The first birthday without them. The first Christmas. The first school holiday. The first time you go to do the thing you used to do with them and they are not there.
Each first is a wave. The waves do not always come on the days you expect them. A song in a shop will sometimes do it. A smell. A turn in the road.
Mark the days you know are going to be hard. Do not pretend you will be fine. Plan something small that holds you. A walk. A meal with one person who knew them. An afternoon off.
The second year
The second year is often harder than the first. The shock has gone. The casseroles are not arriving any more. The world has moved on, and the grief has not.
The work of the second year is to make room for the grief in a life that is also moving on. It does not have to be one or the other. Both can be true at once.
The long arc

By year five, year ten, the grief has changed shape again. The parent is now part of how you move through the world. You hear them in your own voice. You see them in your face. You do the thing they used to do without thinking.
The loud waves come more rarely. When they come, they are still real. They are not a sign that you are not healing. They are a sign that the love is still in you.
What helps
Talk about them. Say their name. Tell the stories.
Mark the days. A candle, a meal, a walk, a phone call to someone who knew them.
Be in your body. The grief lives there as much as in the mind.
Let other people in. Not everyone, but the few who can sit with it.
Be patient with the long arc. The grief takes the time it takes.
Where this reading comes from

Original to homemade.education. The continuing-bonds frame is from the work of Klass, Silverman, and Nickman in 1996. The long-arc shape comes from later grief research and from the writing of people who have lived through the loss.
